queries.query: Given the structural capabilities of Light, Medium, and Heavy USAR teams, how does the capacity for lifting loads manually and mechanically scale across the classifications?

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the specific lifting capacity requirements for Medium search and rescue teams?
Medium teams are required to possess equipment capable of lifting loads up to 1 metric tonne using manual methods and up to 12 metric tonnes using mechanical methods. Furthermore, they must maintain equipment specifically designed for lifting and lowering operations that includes necessary accessories for anchoring, securing, moving, and dragging loads up to a capacity of 12 metric tonnes. Essential equipment for these teams also includes rope systems intended for hauling and anchoring—supported by appropriate accessories—as well as an assortment of bars and levers for manipulating light objects and a comprehensive supply of cribbing materials for structural stabilization.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the structural capability requirements for a Medium USAR team versus a Heavy USAR team?
A Medium USAR Team must be capable of conducting search and rescue operations in collapsed or failed structures constructed of heavy wood, reinforced masonry, lightweight steel, wood frame, and other lightweight construction styles. In addition to these capabilities, a Heavy USAR Team must also demonstrate the capacity to conduct search and rescue operations in two separate locations simultaneously involving collapsed or failed structures made of reinforced concrete or steel-frame construction.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the lifting capacity standards for Heavy search and rescue teams?
Heavy teams must be equipped to handle significantly larger loads, requiring manual lifting capabilities up to 2.5 metric tonnes and mechanical lifting capabilities up to 20 metric tonnes. Beyond these standard mechanical and manual lifting capacities, Heavy teams must also field specialized equipment for lifting and lowering operations, along with a suite of accessories configured for anchoring, securing, moving, and dragging loads that exceed 20 metric tonnes. In addition to these primary systems, Heavy teams must demonstrate proficiency in safe manual lifting techniques and maintain an inventory that includes an assortment of bars and levers for lighter lifting tasks, as well as adequate cribbing supplies to ensure load security and site safety.

queries.response:
Light teams have manual lifting up to 1 metric tonne and mechanical up to 12 metric tonnes. Medium teams require manual lifting up to 1 metric tonne and mechanical up to 12 metric tonnes. Heavy teams require manual lifting up to 2.5 metric tonnes and mechanical up to 20 metric tonnes.

queries.query: If an UNDAC team discovers a chemical spill during an earthquake response, what specific steps must the non-expert team members take, and how does this relate to the Environmental Emergencies Coordination Cell?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What preparatory steps must team members take immediately upon receiving an UNDAC alert?
Upon receiving an UNDAC alert, team members must initiate a series of proactive steps. First, they must evaluate their personal state of health; if there are any doubts regarding existing illnesses, injuries, or mental health status, they must not make themselves available for deployment until these issues are resolved, and they must inform OCHA accordingly. Second, they must ensure their individual medical kit is thoroughly prepared and packed, making sure to include any necessary prescription medication or supplies that might not be available at the deployment location. Finally, they need to check local health threats and requirements, updating their medical kit as appropriate and starting any necessary prophylactic treatment.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What key components must be covered when introducing an UNDAC team to partners or authorities?
The introduction must include a brief overview of the UNDAC concept as OCHA’s rapid response tool, citing relevant past missions, major recent emergencies, and prior work within the host country. Each team member's specific experience and the capacities of deployed support partners should be presented, preferably via a handout. The team must articulate its value-added tasks, including coordinating initial needs assessments and analysis, supporting information management, and establishing coordination links between international responders (NGOs, donors, foreign military, private sector, and bilateral teams). They must also outline how they will establish functional relationships with national/local authorities, ensure interface with international responses through the OSOCC or liaison officers, support inter-cluster coordination, and reinforce humanitarian structures at the disaster site. Additionally, the team should introduce the OSOCC concept, outline the initial Plan of Action (PoA) and its integration with wider OCHA operations, and provide an overview of additional OCHA services available, such as environmental impact assessments.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should an UNDAC team establish assessment coordination in an emergency?
Assessment coordination requires several specific actions: Communicate the purpose and capacity of the A&A function clearly to the team and OSOCC. Explain the benefits of coordination, including the synchronization of planned assessments, shared analysis, the reduction of assessment fatigue, and the maximization of available resources. Ensure A&A is a recurring agenda item at coordination meetings. In larger emergencies, establish a dedicated assessment working group; in smaller ones, integrate into existing platforms such as IM working groups or general coordination meetings. Finally, implement an e-mail and assessment registry to facilitate the efficient collection, storage, and sharing of assessment findings across organizations.

queries.response:
Team members must immediately assess the local health threats and update their medical kits. They need to check local health threats and requirements and start any necessary prophylactic treatment. This relates to the Environmental Emergencies Coordination Cell by ensuring appropriate medical preparedness for environmental hazards.

queries.query: Compare the primary operational responsibilities of the USAR Coordination Cell (UCC) Manager with those of the Reception/Departure Centre (RDC) Manager during the initial phases of a disaster.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the primary responsibilities of the USAR Coordination Cell (UCC) in relation to the LEMA and arriving USAR teams?
The UCC is a specialized cell within the OSOCC operations function designed to support the LEMA in planning and tasking international USAR teams during the initial life-saving phase. Its core responsibilities include: facilitating the establishment of Bases of Operations for USAR teams and briefing them on camp protocols; facilitating worksite assignments in coordination with the LEMA; conducting cultural and safety briefings; and coordinating further needs assessments with USAR team support. The UCC acts as a bridge between the LEMA and international responders, ensuring that incoming teams are integrated effectively. Additionally, the UCC must manage the administrative and logistical aspects of incoming resources, including gathering incident information, recording team fact sheets, and identifying critical local assets such as heavy machinery (cranes, loaders), fuel, materials, and support staff like interpreters or guides. The UCC must also maintain active communication links with the Reception Departure Centre (RDC) and the Virtual OSOCC (VOSOCC) to ensure operational continuity and situational awareness.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the primary function of the USAR Coordination Cell (UCC) and how is it staffed?
The UCC is a specialized cell within the Operations Function of an OSOCC. Its primary purpose is to strengthen and assist with the coordination of international USAR teams during the critical rescue phase of a disaster. Given the high speed of operations and the narrow 'rescue window,' the UCC must be staffed by a combination of UNDAC team members and personnel from international USAR teams. The UCC provides specialized technical support to the Local Emergency Management Authority (LEMA) for the planning and tasking of resources. To ensure sufficient operational capacity, international relief teams are expected to second a member to the UCC for liaison duties upon arrival. If USAR teams arrive in an affected country before the UNDAC team, those teams are mandated by INSARAG global requirements to establish and operate both the UCC and the Reception Departure Centre (RDC) until the UNDAC team assumes these responsibilities.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the three core responsibilities of the UCC and UNDAC team during the USAR demobilization phase?
The UNDAC team and the UCC assist with the USAR demobilization process through three primary actions: 1) Establishing a departure schedule, for which USAR teams must submit a standardized request form detailing their departure information (available in the UNDAC Mission Software and INSARAG Guidelines). 2) Determining and organizing logistical requirements; the UCC/OSOCC must cooperate with the LEMA to coordinate transportation from the affected area to the departure point, carefully scheduling movements to prevent bottlenecks or the straining of limited local transport resources. 3) Converting the RDC (Reception/Departure Centre) into a Departure Centre to manage the debriefing of outgoing international teams. Additionally, the OSOCC is responsible for coordinating the donation of equipment by USAR teams to the host Government for continued relief operations.

queries.response:
The USAR Coordination Cell (UCC) Manager focuses on planning and tasking international USAR teams, including establishing Bases of Operations, briefing teams, and coordinating needs assessments. The Reception/Departure Centre (RDC) Manager's role involves managing the reception and departure logistics for incoming and outgoing international teams. The UCC is primarily focused on the initial life-saving phase coordination, while the RDC manages the flow of personnel and resources during the reception and departure stages.

queries.query: How do the four core components of inter-organizational trust influence the way an UNDAC team should initially engage with local partner agencies and establish coordination?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the four components of inter-organizational trust in a humanitarian context?
Inter-organizational trust is built on four distinct pillars: 1) Goodwill: The judgment of others as friends and companions. 2) Competence: The perceived ability of other organizations to execute required tasks and achieve results. 3) Commitments: The consistency of behavior with established contractual agreements. 4) Expediency: The trust based on the necessity of achieving goals quickly (swift trust). To build this trust, the UNDAC team should prioritize simple, non-controversial, and functional tasks, such as elementary information sharing, before attempting to coordinate on more complex or polarizing issues. They should also leverage existing social networks and sectoral interests (e.g., USAR or specific clusters) and rally partners around the development of common strategic plans or joint funding appeals to solidify cooperative relationships.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What key components must be covered when introducing an UNDAC team to partners or authorities?
The introduction must include a brief overview of the UNDAC concept as OCHA’s rapid response tool, citing relevant past missions, major recent emergencies, and prior work within the host country. Each team member's specific experience and the capacities of deployed support partners should be presented, preferably via a handout. The team must articulate its value-added tasks, including coordinating initial needs assessments and analysis, supporting information management, and establishing coordination links between international responders (NGOs, donors, foreign military, private sector, and bilateral teams). They must also outline how they will establish functional relationships with national/local authorities, ensure interface with international responses through the OSOCC or liaison officers, support inter-cluster coordination, and reinforce humanitarian structures at the disaster site. Additionally, the team should introduce the OSOCC concept, outline the initial Plan of Action (PoA) and its integration with wider OCHA operations, and provide an overview of additional OCHA services available, such as environmental impact assessments.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the four components of inter-organizational trust in a humanitarian emergency environment?
In an emergency, building swift trust is essential for effective collaboration. According to the manual, trust originates from four specific aspects: 1) Goodwill/Friendship: Trust based on the judgment of the other party as a companion. 2) Competence: Trust based on the perceived ability of others to successfully carry out tasks and deliver results. 3) Commitments: Trust based on consistent behavior aligned with established contractual agreements. 4) Swiftness: Trust based on expediency, driven by the urgent necessity to accomplish goals quickly.

queries.response:
Goodwill, Competence, Commitments, and Expediency form the basis of inter-organizational trust. UNDAC teams should prioritize simple, non-controversial tasks like elementary information sharing to build this trust initially. Leveraging existing social networks and rallying partners around joint strategic plans will solidify cooperative relationships.

queries.query: If the Local Emergency Management Authority (LEMA) tasks a USAR team to an operation that contradicts the INSARAG triage scale of survivability, how should the team and the UCC respond based on the guidelines?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should the UCC handle priority setting between INSARAG methodology and LEMA directives?
The USAR community operates based on a triage scale of survivability, prioritizing missions where there is the highest probability of saving lives. However, the LEMA may have broader political, social, or strategic reasons for assigning priorities that deviate from this technical concept. In these situations, USAR teams are required to work as directed by the LEMA. Despite this requirement for compliance, USAR teams should actively advocate for the utilization of standard INSARAG methodology. The UCC plays a vital role here by acting as the intermediary to ensure that the LEMA understands the technical rationale behind the survivability-based approach, while ensuring that all operational activities remain fully integrated within the host nation's command structure. The ultimate goal is to balance professional rescue standards with the host-nation authority and the specific priority needs of the affected country's government.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-3.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should the UCC Manager coordinate with LEMA representatives regarding incident management?
The UCC Manager must ensure a continuous communication link with LEMA (Local Emergency Management Agency) representatives to align on USAR (Urban Search and Rescue) objectives and accomplishments for every operational period. The manager is tasked with obtaining information from LEMA regarding national USAR teams to strategically allocate international teams based on their comparative strengths, specific capabilities, and available resources. Based on these LEMA priorities, the manager develops and disseminates operational objectives for USAR operations. Additionally, the manager must identify and report information gaps—such as missing, unreliable, or contradictory data—to the OSOCC Manager, and provide recommendations for additional assessments to bridge these gaps. This coordination ensures that international operations remain in line with local priorities and capacities.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How does the UCC Manager interact with LEMA during USAR operations?
The UCC Manager ensures a robust communication link with LEMA representatives to align on objectives and accomplishments for each operational period. They must extract information from LEMA regarding national USAR teams to ensure international USAR teams are allocated effectively based on available resources, capabilities, and comparative strengths. The Manager develops and disseminates operational objectives for USAR operations that are strictly in line with LEMA's identified priorities. Additionally, the UCC Manager reports directly to the OSOCC Manager (or OSOCC Operations Manager) and is responsible for identifying information gaps—such as missing, contradictory, or unreliable data—and providing professional recommendations to LEMA for additional assessment requirements.

queries.response:
The USAR team should work as directed by the LEMA, but should advocate for the use of the INSARAG methodology. The UCC must act as an intermediary to ensure the LEMA understands the technical rationale behind the survivability-based approach. The goal is to balance professional rescue standards with the host-nation authority and specific priority needs of the affected country's government.

queries.query: Describe the complete process for converting Degrees and Decimal Minutes (DDD° MM.mmm) into Degrees Decimal (DDD.dddd°) and explain why this specific format is mandated by INSARAG.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the manual process for converting Degrees, Minutes, and Seconds (DDD° MM' SS.s") to Degrees Decimal (DDD.dddd°)?
The conversion from Degrees, Minutes, and Seconds (DMS) to Degrees Decimal requires a three-part calculation: Degrees + (Minutes / 60) + (Seconds / 3600). To execute this, take the degrees value, add the minutes divided by 60, and add the seconds divided by 3600. For instance, using 33°59'51.0"S, the calculation is 33 + (59 / 60) + (51.0 / 3600). This breaks down to 33 + 0.9833 + 0.0142, equaling 33.9975. If the coordinate is in the Southern hemisphere or involves a Western longitude, you must prefix the resulting value with a minus sign (-) to represent the location accurately in the Degrees Decimal format, resulting in -33.9975 in this example.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-2.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the procedure for converting Degrees, Minutes, and Seconds (DDD°MM'SS.s") to Degrees Decimal (DDD.dddd°)?
To convert Degrees, Minutes, and Seconds (DMS) to Degrees Decimal, use the formula: Degrees + (Minutes / 60) + (Seconds / 3600). First, retain the Degrees whole number. Second, divide the Minutes value by 60 to get the first decimal component. Third, divide the Seconds value by 3600 to get the second decimal component. Sum all three values to reach the final Degrees Decimal format. If the location is in the Southern Hemisphere, the final result must be expressed as a negative number. For example, with 33°59'51.0"S, calculate 33 + (59 / 60) + (51.0 / 3600). This yields 33 + 0.9833 + 0.0142. Summing these results in 33.9975, which is then adjusted for the Southern Hemisphere to arrive at the final coordinate of -33.9975.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-3.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How do I convert Degrees and Decimal Minutes (DDD° MM.mmm') into Decimal Degrees (DDD.dddd°)?
To convert from Degrees and Decimal Minutes to Decimal Degrees, follow the mathematical conversion formula: Degrees Decimal = Degrees + (Minutes / 60). For example, if you are given the coordinate 33°59.850'S, you identify the degrees as 33 and the minutes as 59.850. First, divide the minutes by 60 (59.850 / 60 = 0.9975). Then, add this result to the degrees (33 + 0.9975 = 33.9975). Because the coordinate is in the Southern hemisphere, you must apply a negative value, resulting in -33.9975. This conversion process ensures that coordinate data provided in standard nautical or topographic formats is rendered compatible with decimal-based digital navigation systems and GPS software, which require consistent decimal notation for accurate plotting and target acquisition.

queries.response: Degrees Decimal = Degrees + (Minutes / 60). This process involves dividing the minutes by 60 and adding the result to the degrees. For Southern Hemisphere locations, the final result must be negative. INSARAG mandates this format for compatibility with digital navigation systems and GPS software for accurate plotting and target acquisition.

queries.query: What is the difference in how the "Staff only", "Team only", and "Publicly displayed" information boards are utilized within an established Reception/Departure Centre (RDC)?

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-3.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Which specific information displays are restricted to 'Staff only' within the RDC and UCC?
The information displays designated for 'Staff only' are categorized into two primary functional areas. First, for both the RDC (Reception/Departure Centre) and UCC (Urban Search and Rescue Coordination Cell), 'Staff only' displays include: Coordination staff information, Logistics available, Logistics requests, and Resource tracking. Second, specific displays are restricted based on the operational venue. Incoming USAR (Urban Search and Rescue) teams must be displayed in both the RDC and UCC. Incoming medical teams and incoming relief teams are restricted to the RDC. Finally, airport information is restricted to RDC operations. These designations ensure that sensitive logistical, resource, and incoming personnel data are managed exclusively by authorized coordination staff to maintain operational security and situational control during relief efforts.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-2.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Which specific informational displays are designated as 'Staff only' for RDC and UCC operations?
The 'Staff only' category comprises several critical operational tracking and coordination displays used at both the Reception/Departure Centre (RDC) and the Urban Search and Rescue Coordination Cell (UCC). These displays include Incoming USAR teams (RDC only), Incoming medical teams (RDC only), Incoming relief teams (RDC only), Airport information (RDC only), and Outstanding issues (RDC and UCC). Furthermore, the following displays are restricted to staff at both RDC and UCC locations: Coordination staff information, Logistics available, Logistics requests, and Resource tracking. These displays facilitate internal management, administrative oversight, and logistical planning that should not be accessible to non-staff personnel or the general public to maintain operational security and effective resource distribution.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-2.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Which information displays are classified as 'Publicly displayed' within the RDC and UCC frameworks?
Publicly displayed information is designed for broad situational awareness and logistical orientation for all arriving or departing personnel. These displays include Airport facilities (RDC only), Safety and security (RDC and UCC), Infrastructure status (RDC and UCC), Host government information (RDC and UCC), Information sources (RDC and UCC), Weather Forecast (RDC and UCC), Briefing Schedule (RDC and UCC), and Reporting Schedule (RDC and UCC). These displays serve to keep all stakeholders informed about essential environmental and procedural conditions. By providing public access to infrastructure updates, security protocols, and weather forecasts, the RDC and UCC maintain transparency and ensure that all incoming teams and relief organizations are aligned with the current operational environment, reporting requirements, and the safety measures established by the host government.

queries.response:
Staff only information includes coordination staff, logistics, requests, and resource tracking. Team only information restricts incoming USAR, medical, and relief teams to the RDC. Publicly displayed information covers airport facilities, safety, infrastructure status, host government info, weather, briefing, and reporting schedules.

queries.query: In the context of the MIRA process, how does the "good-enough" approach during Phase II differ from the data collection expectations in Phase III of a large-scale emergency?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the recommended sampling approach for a MIRA during the initial stage of a disaster?
The MIRA employs a 'good-enough' approach during the initial phase of a disaster response. This methodology recommends the use of a purposive sampling method. Crucially, the assessment should be focused at the community level by engaging with key informants, rather than attempting to conduct household-level surveys. This approach is specifically designed to facilitate the timely generation of actionable findings with limited resources, ensuring that strategic decision-makers receive immediate situational awareness rather than waiting for the publication of a detailed report, which is often subject to delays that render the information obsolete in a rapidly evolving crisis environment.

queries.response:
The "good-enough" approach in Phase II focuses on timely, actionable findings for strategic decision-makers. Phase III expectations shift towards more detailed, comprehensive data collection to support sustained recovery planning.

1. Phase II: Purposive sampling at the community level for immediate situational awareness.
2. Phase III: Detailed data collection for long-term recovery strategy formulation.
3. Phase II goal: Generate actionable findings quickly.
4. Phase III goal: Support sustained recovery planning.

queries.query: Explain how the UNDAC team coordinates with the World Food Programme (WFP) and the Logistics Cluster from the initial arrival of the Logistics Response Team (LRT) to the execution of the logistics program.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the procedure for activating the Global Logistics Cluster?
The decision to activate the Global Logistics Cluster is driven by field operation requirements. The Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC), in consultation with the Humanitarian Country Team (HCT), is responsible for identifying the need for a cluster based on an assessment of gaps and existing response capacities of the host government, local authorities, and international actors. The activation process involves: 1) The RC/HC informing the Emergency Relief Coordinator (ERC); 2) The Global Logistics Cluster lead determining the necessary response nature; and 3) Establishing country-level clusters tailored to the scale of the need. Often, a Logistics Response Team (LRT) is deployed to evaluate the situation. If the LRT determines activation is unnecessary, they report this to the RC/HC and no country-level cluster is established. If activated, the LRT initiates operations and coordinates with UNDAC to establish a common situational picture.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the role of the Logistics Response Team (LRT) during an emergency?
The Logistics Response Team (LRT) is a specialized unit deployed to the field to perform a critical assessment of the logistics situation. Their primary objectives are to determine if the activation of a Logistics Cluster is required and to identify what specific logistics support is necessary within the country. The LRT is composed of experts from various organizations, potentially including staff from the Global Logistics Cluster Support Cell in Rome. When deployed, the LRT frequently collaborates with UNDAC teams to synthesize a shared understanding of the operational environment. Field logisticians are encouraged to contact LRT members early, as their technical input is vital during the initial phases of the emergency. If the LRT concludes that Logistics Cluster activities are not required, they report this finding to the RC/HC. If activation is deemed appropriate, the LRT serves as the catalyst for initiating cluster operations.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should an UNDAC team finalize and utilize its Plan of Action (PoA) during execution?
The Plan of Action (PoA) is finalized following the team's initial contact with the Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC), the mandatory security briefing, and other essential early meetings. Once these inputs are integrated, the team must affirm or adjust its mission objectives based on the current reality on the ground and available options. After finalizing the PoA, the team is expected to commence activities immediately. However, execution is dynamic; the PoA must not remain static. The team must regularly review the PoA as a 'guiding beacon' to maintain focus on overall mission objectives amid changing priorities and situational shifts. Regular operational reviews should involve checking for new situational developments, adjusting team organization (such as OCHA surge integration or addressing expertise gaps), evaluating coordination support needs, closing information gaps, updating security and communication protocols, and incorporating the latest disaster statistics. This iterative process ensures that the daily work programme remains relevant, aligned with the Cornerstones of the UNDAC methodology, and responsive to the evolving disaster context.

queries.response:
The LRT coordinates with UNDAC teams to synthesize a shared understanding of the operational environment. The LRT determines if a Logistics Cluster activation is necessary, which informs subsequent coordination. If activation is deemed appropriate, the LRT initiates cluster operations and coordinates with UNDAC.

queries.query: Compare the operational focus and meeting types required during the "Storming" phase of team development versus those needed in the "Performing" phase.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What information should the UNDAC team analyze and report to support humanitarian situational awareness?
The UNDAC team must ensure that data gathered through EMT coordination is utilized for broader situational analysis and priority setting. Key focus areas include: 1) The impact on the national health system. 2) The status of health facilities, specifically comparing the number of functioning facilities versus those that are damaged or non-functioning. 3) An inventory of all deployed national and international EMTs, including their specific numbers and types. 4) Any evidence of disease outbreaks. Furthermore, the UNDAC team is responsible for sharing logistical and operational arrangements, such as transport and refueling details, and ensuring regular information exchange between the EMTCC and the OSOCC A&A (Assessment and Analysis) Cell.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the purpose of the consultative leadership style and in what team phase is it most effective?
The consultative leadership style focuses on achieving results by leading group discussions, asking probing questions, and actively motivating team members to volunteer for specific responsibilities. The primary objective is to build consensus around decisions, which fosters a sense of commitment to team processes and builds institutional trust. The most essential skill required for this style is facilitation. The manual identifies this style as particularly effective during the 'storming' stage of team development, where interpersonal friction or process ambiguity may occur. However, because the consultative process is inherently time-consuming, it should not be used exclusively; leaders must develop the flexibility to transition to other styles when speed or decisive action is required to meet mission objectives.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should a team navigate the Storming phase of development?
The Storming phase occurs when members hold conflicting opinions regarding operational procedures. Because this stage involves tension, individuals anxious about conflict may find it challenging. Effective teams recognize that this tension is a necessary, natural phase of development rather than a sign of failure. To successfully navigate Storming, members must practice active listening and work collaboratively to establish an agreed-upon way forward. If members fail to resolve these conflicts or attempt to force their individual opinions over others, the team risks disintegration. Successfully navigating this phase is essential for transitioning from a collection of individuals into a unified team capable of achieving collective leadership objectives.

queries.response:
Storming phase focus: Resolving conflicting opinions on operational procedures through active listening and collaborative establishment of an agreed way forward.
Performing phase focus: Executing tasks efficiently and achieving mission objectives with established processes.
Meeting types: Storming requires discussions to address friction and build consensus. Performing requires focused coordination for execution.
Key difference: Storming is about conflict resolution; Performing is about high-level task execution.

queries.query: How does the OSOCC's mandate to act as a "provider of last resort" apply to the activation and functionality of the Logistics Cluster?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the primary responsibilities of the Logistics Cluster upon activation during emergency response operations?
Upon activation, the Logistics Cluster is responsible for coordinating the logistics sector, managing information, and providing services where necessary. Its core function is to fill gaps in logistics capacity and coordinate essential services, acting as the 'provider of last resort.' The cluster enables the humanitarian community to leverage shared assets, competencies, and technical aptitudes from the lead agency and participating organizations. Operations vary in scale based on the emergency needs; they range from information sharing—such as providing infrastructure assessments, corridor and port coordination, transporter and rate details, customs information, and equipment supplier data—to active provision of common air, ocean, and overland transport and storage solutions. The overall goal is to ensure system-wide preparedness and the technical capacity to respond effectively to humanitarian emergencies.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Under what specific conditions should a USAR medical provider consider performing an amputation in the field?
Amputation should be strictly reserved as a procedure of last resort. It should only be considered when the following two conditions are met: First, when hazards present an immediate and direct threat to the life of the victim or the USAR team members. Second, when an amputation is determined to offer a higher probability of survival for the victim compared to attempting resuscitation while they remain entrapped within the collapsed structure. The text explicitly notes that USAR medical providers should not attempt to use standard limb salvage score criteria, as these are designed for controlled operating theatre environments and are unrealistic to apply in field rescue scenarios.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the protocol for managing severe bleeding when professional medical help is delayed?
First, use sterile gauze to apply firm, direct pressure to the wound. Once bleeding ceases, secure the gauze in place with tape; do not remove the gauze, as this may dislodge clots and cause bleeding to restart. If bleeding is uncontrollable and professional help is hours away, a tourniquet may be applied to the affected extremity as a last resort. Operators must be aware that tourniquet application carries a substantial risk of losing the extremity.

The Logistics Cluster's mandate is to coordinate the logistics sector and provide essential services, acting as the 'provider of last resort.' It enables the humanitarian community to leverage shared assets and competencies from lead agencies. queries.query: Describe the step-by-step procedure for operating a BGAN terminal, including environmental considerations and the specific bandwidth management protocols required.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What operational constraints and bandwidth management protocols should be observed when using BGAN terminals?
BGAN systems have a limited bandwidth of up to 0.5 Mbps, which is shared among all connected users and further shared via 'spot-beams' among users in the same geographical area. Given high operational costs—reaching up to US$6 per megabyte of data and US$16 per minute for streaming—strict bandwidth management is required. Operators must disable automatic computer updates and avoid non-essential activities such as watching video, downloading large email attachments, using social media feeds, or using Skype unless strictly necessary. These measures are critical, particularly in large-scale emergencies where limited satellite capacity is shared across multiple actors. When BGAN capacity or cost is prohibitive, personnel should evaluate existing local infrastructure or, for expanding missions, request a VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal) through partners like the IHP or TSF, noting that VSAT requires specially trained personnel for installation.

queries.response:
Follow these steps for BGAN terminal operation:
1. Disable automatic computer updates.
2. Avoid non-essential activities like video watching or large downloads.
3. Manage bandwidth by being mindful of the 0.5 Mbps shared capacity and spot-beam sharing.
4. Evaluate local infrastructure or request VSAT if BGAN capacity/cost is prohibitive.

queries.query: How does the Humanitarian Programme Cycle (HPC) dictate the evolution of the Flash Appeal into a long-term Humanitarian Response Plan (HRP), and what is the maximum duration for each?

chunks.content:
P: What is the Humanitarian Programme Cycle (HPC) according to the IASC?
The International Association of Search and Rescue (IASC) has agreed that the Humanitarian Programme Cycle (HPC) guides international humanitarian response. It is defined as a coordinated series of actions designed to help prepare for, manage, and deliver the overall humanitarian response. The cycle is composed of five distinct elements that must be coordinated seamlessly, ensuring that each step logically builds upon the previous one and progresses naturally to the next.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the nature of the A&A cycle during a humanitarian response?
A&A processes are not one-off events; they are iterative in nature. Each subsequent assessment must build upon the previous one, as the information requirements shift from general observations to more detailed, sector-specific, and long-term data as the response develops. For UNDAC teams, initial reports will typically rely on a limited number of existing sources. As the response progresses, these initial reports should be complemented by targeted field visits to collect primary data and direct observations once sufficient secondary data has been collated to guide those visits.

queries.response:
The Humanitarian Programme Cycle (HPC) guides the coordinated series of actions for humanitarian response. It dictates the progression from preparation and response phases to ensure a logical flow of aid. The cycle itself does not specify maximum durations for the transition from Flash Appeal to HRP.

queries.query: If an Emergency Medical Team (EMT) arrives unannounced and fails to register at the EMTCC, what is the protocol for the UNDAC team, and who holds the ultimate authority to sanction them?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the role of the UNDAC team in the activation of the EMTCC?
The UNDAC team serves as a critical bridge for operational coordination. Their primary tasks include: 1) Linking with the WHO country office to determine the status of EMTCC activation and overall preparedness for establishing coordination mechanisms. This information should also be cross-referenced with the Local Emergency Management Authority (LEMA). 2) If the team identifies a need for greater support, they must escalate requests to the WHO EMT Secretariat or the UNDAC mission focal point in OCHA Geneva. 3) Identifying team members specifically trained in EMTCC functions who can be deployed to assist in the activation and operational management of the EMTCC during the emergency.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Who is the primary authority responsible for managing non-compliant Emergency Medical Teams (EMTs)?
The Ministry of Health (MoH) and the EMT Coordination Cell (EMTCC) are the sole institutions empowered to make decisions regarding the management of non-compliant EMTs. While UNDAC members may support the process, their role is restricted to disseminating information regarding the coordination process, directing all EMTs to that process, and ensuring that any reports regarding the quality of care or activities of an EMT are properly funneled to the EMT coordination mechanism for verification. Additionally, UNDAC may assist by staffing a contact center to handle EMT enquiries if required, but the actual administrative or disciplinary decisions remain exclusively within the jurisdiction of the MoH/EMTCC.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the expected protocol for managing military Emergency Medical Teams (EMTs) during a disaster response?
In accordance with established protocols, military EMTs should, as a general rule, be referred to the Emergency Medical Teams Coordination Cell (EMTCC) operating under the leadership of the Ministry of Health (MoH). This practice is intended to maintain a unified EMT coordination platform. Exceptions to this protocol only apply if the host Government makes a specific decision to deviate from this arrangement. This oversight is intended to ensure that all medical assets, regardless of their military status, function within the centralized coordination mechanism to minimize gaps in service and ensure adherence to national standards.

queries.response:
The UNDAC team's role is to disseminate information regarding the coordination process and direct the EMT to the EMTCC. The Ministry of Health (MoH) and the EMT Coordination Cell (EMTCC) hold the ultimate authority to sanction non-compliant EMTs.

queries.query: According to the guidelines, how should a facilitator use Open Neutral, Closed Neutral, Open Biased, and Closed Biased questions to steer a stalled coordination meeting?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the tactical differences between various types of questioning techniques in a facilitation setting?
Facilitators use specific question types to control conversation flow: 1) Open neutral questions (e.g., 'What options do we have?') are best at the start to generate a wide range of ideas. 2) Closed neutral questions (e.g., 'Do we have an agreement?') are effective at the end of a session to force a decision. 3) Open biased questions (e.g., 'How can we make this happen?') steer the group toward progress without limiting the answer to a single possibility, while implicitly signaling that success is expected. 4) Closed biased questions (e.g., 'Don't you think we should...?' ) should be avoided as they polarize the group and undermine the facilitator's neutrality and objectivity.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the administrative and procedural best practices for conducting effective, high-stakes coordination meetings?
Administrative best practices include circulating an attendance sheet with contact information for all participants to facilitate follow-up communication. Procedurally, the facilitator should maintain a firm grasp on the meeting flow. When discussions become unproductive or tense, the facilitator must avoid polarized language. Instead, they should employ techniques to move the group from a problem-oriented mindset to an outcome-oriented focus. This includes asking probing questions that force participants to take responsibility for desired end states. Facilitators should also be prepared to explore the underlying assumptions and mental models of participants who hold strongly to specific opinions. By using a mix of open and neutral questions, the facilitator can generate options at the start of a discussion, while saving closed, neutral questions for the end to finalize agreements and ensure the group remains committed to the established trajectory.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should a facilitator manage meetings that drift into inappropriate levels of detail?
To maintain meeting focus, the facilitator must stay on top of the conversation and ensure the discussion aligns with the agenda item and the intended outcome. If a group strays from the agreed-upon level of complexity, the facilitator must clarify the situation before attempting to solve problems. It is necessary to remind the group of the level previously agreed upon by using reframing questions, such as asking if an operational detail will advance the strategy, or requesting a return to the strategic level. If a group persistently diverts, the facilitator should formally ask the group to make a conscious decision to shift the topic, or propose that the issue be closed or 'parked' for later discussion.

queries.response:
Open neutral questions generate ideas at the start, closed neutral questions finalize decisions at the end, and open biased questions steer toward progress without limiting options. Closed biased questions should be avoided as they risk polarizing the group.

queries.query: What specific operational data points are required to populate both the Team Availability List and the Priority Assignment List during the worksite prioritization process?

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-3.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Which operational variables must be considered when creating a Priority Assignment List?
When filling in column 3 to finalize the Priority Assignment List, you must incorporate operational intelligence that may influence the sequence of deployments. Key considerations include formal directives provided by LEMA (Local Emergency Management Agency), current rubble safety assessments, and existing security issues at or near the sites. Additionally, you must evaluate the proximity of the worksite to other assigned locations and any physical access restraints that might impede the movement or efficiency of USAR teams. These variables ensure that the sequencing of tasks is both safe and logistically sound.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-2.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What criteria are used to rank teams on the Team Availability List?
To compile the Team Availability List (Column 2 of the Team Assignment Plan), teams should be ranked based on their shortest estimated time until availability. When assessing this, the following metrics must be documented for every team: their Base of Operations (BoO), their current working location, and their operational capacity, categorized as High (H), Medium (M), or Low (L). For teams currently engaged in an active site, the estimated time of completion and subsequent availability must be recorded. Additionally, this list must account for international teams currently in transit to the country, noting their estimated arrival time. This systematic tracking ensures the most rapid deployment of resources to high-priority sites.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should I prioritize worksites in the Team Assignment Plan during the initial phase?
To order worksites correctly in column 1 of the Team Assignment Plan, you must utilize data derived from previous ASR-2 (Assessment, Search, and Rescue) missions. The prioritization must be based strictly on victim-related information, specifically the triage categories identified at the site and the total number of victims reported. By inputting this data, you establish a primary sequence that reflects the human life safety requirements before integrating logistical or secondary operational constraints.

queries.response:
For the Team Availability List, document Base of Operations (BoO), current working location, and operational capacity (High, Medium, or Low). For the Priority Assignment List, use victim-related information, specifically triage categories and the total number of victims reported.

They serve as the primary advisor to the Designated Official (DO) and the Security Management Team (SMT) regarding all security matters. As the senior security official at a duty station, they are held accountable to UNDSS. This structure ensures that specialized security expertise is integrated into the decision-making process at the highest level of the duty station's hierarchy.

queries.query: How do the requirements for a Structural Engineer on a USAR team differ from those of a Rigging Specialist regarding building stabilization and heavy lifting?

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the specific operational limits and rigging requirements for a Rigging Specialist regarding building stabilization and lifting capacity?
Rigging Specialists must develop rigging plans and procedures while coordinating between USAR personnel and local heavy equipment operators. For Light USAR teams, they must stabilize building components and apply lifting techniques for loads up to 1 tonne (manual) and 5 tonnes (mechanical/slings/cranes). For Medium USAR teams, the capacity increases to 1 metric tonne (manual) and 12 metric tonnes (mechanical). Additionally, all Rigging Specialists must be proficient in working safely at heights and performing rope rescues for patients located up to 10 metres above or below the primary work site. They are responsible for assessing the overall capacity and necessity of all construction-related equipment deployed at the incident site.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the specific operational prerequisites and responsibilities for a Rigging Specialist in a USAR team?
A Rigging Specialist must possess knowledge of heavy construction equipment capabilities and methods for structure demolition and construction. Their responsibilities include a comprehensive understanding of USAR team disciplines, tactics, and safety considerations, alongside the practical application of technologies and disaster environment hazard mitigation. Key operational duties include proficiency in heavy rigging, such as calculating lifting capacity, applying lifting engineering principles, installing anchor systems, and utilizing shoring methods and materials. They are also responsible for using universal hand signals for heavy equipment operation, coordinating with engineers, and developing practical solutions for tactical rigging and lifting problems. Furthermore, the specialist must demonstrate interpersonal skills, specifically in communication, cooperation, and coordination.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the core technical duties and specialized focus areas for a Structural Engineer in a USAR operation?
Structural Engineers across all classifications (Light, Medium, or Heavy) are tasked with identifying structural types, assessing specific damage and associated hazards, and structural monitoring. They are responsible for designing, inspecting, and supervising the construction of structural shores to stabilize environments. These engineers must be proficient in recognizing risks inherent to specific construction types and understanding the potential consequences of structural failure. Furthermore, they are required to identify various construction materials and perform triage on collapse patterns to predict and evaluate void formations. They must possess a comprehensive inventory of tools, supplies, and equipment necessary to monitor building stability and execute the design of shoring systems.

queries.response:
Structural Engineers focus on identifying structural types, assessing damage, and designing shores for stabilization. Rigging Specialists focus on developing rigging plans, calculating lifting capacity, and applying lifting techniques for loads. Key differences involve structural assessment and shoring design for Engineers versus load management and rigging execution for Specialists.

queries.query: Compare the procedures and goals of "Defusing" versus "Psychological First Aid" when managing critical incident stress among mission staff.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the core goals and procedural guidelines for conducting a 'defusing' session?
Defusing is a peer-led intervention for staff members exposed to a traumatic event who remain in control of their emotions. It should not be forced upon anyone, especially within the first few hours post-incident. The primary goals are to provide information about the incident, allow for the sharing of perceptions and stress reactions, reinforce mutual support to dispel feelings of uniqueness, and identify which colleagues require professional follow-up services. The session should be facilitated as a free-flowing conversation rather than a rigid interrogation. It is critical to distinguish 'defusing' from 'psychological debriefing,' as the former is designed for non-mental health professionals to help normalize stress responses, whereas debriefing is a specific clinical process conducted by mental health professionals.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the distinction between 'defusing' and 'psychological debriefing'?
In a tactical or humanitarian context, it is critical not to conflate defusing with psychological debriefing. Defusing is an intervention aimed at staff members who have been exposed to a traumatic event and remain in control of their emotions; it focuses on providing info, sharing reactions, and reinforcing mutual support. Conversely, psychological debriefing is a process conducted exclusively by mental health professionals. The manual notes that there is currently no consensus among psychologists regarding the overall effectiveness of formal psychological debriefing, distinguishing it from the peer-supported nature of defusing and Psychological First Aid.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the core personnel management requirements for an operations officer in a disaster response environment?
The personnel management responsibilities for an operations officer include maintaining high levels of communication, cooperation, and coordination within the team. Additionally, they must apply robust human relations techniques, which involve formal negotiation skills and conflict resolution strategies to handle interpersonal or organizational friction. The officer is also responsible for critical incident debriefing to process operational stressors and must prioritize staff welfare throughout the deployment. These functions ensure that team cohesion is maintained and that psychological and professional needs are addressed, which is vital for sustained performance in high-stress disaster zones.

queries.response:
Defusing is a peer-led intervention for staff to manage emotions, focusing on information sharing and mutual support. Psychological First Aid is a specific clinical process conducted by mental health professionals. Defusing aims to normalize stress responses, while debriefing is a clinical process.

queries.query: Detail the specific logistical and spatial requirements (in square meters) needed to establish a base camp for 25 people with 10 vehicles.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the logistical requirements for requesting and establishing an international base camp?
Establishing a large base camp is a complex, resource-intensive logistical operation. Upon determining the requirement, a formal request must be submitted immediately to allow providers, such as the International Humanitarian Partnership (IHP), to initiate preparations. Providers typically dispatch a reconnaissance or liaison team to assess the site and prepare for the camp's arrival. The UNDAC team is often tasked with the initial site selection process. Before the arrival of the camp modules, it is mandatory to secure a formal contract for the use of the selected land. The site selection process is governed by specific capacity calculations: a 1000 m² minimum is required for core common facilities, supplemented by 15 m² per accommodated person, 10 m² per office space, and 15 m² per vehicle. For instance, a facility for 25 people with 10 office spaces and 10 vehicles requires 1575 m².

*   Core common facilities: 1000 m²
*   Accommodations: 15 m² per person (25 people * 15 m² = 375 m²)
*   Office space: 10 m² per office (10 offices * 10 m² = 100 m²)
*   Vehicle space: 15 m² per vehicle (10 vehicles * 15 m² = 150 m²)

queries.query: What is the protocol for integrating regional disaster mechanisms like the ASEAN ERAT or CDEMA RNAT into the UN's OSOCC structure to prevent "silo-thinking"?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Which specific ASEAN regional entities are responsible for disaster management and emergency response?
The ASEAN Agreement on Disaster Management and Emergency Response (AADMER) serves as the legally-binding regional framework. The AHA Centre (ASEAN Coordinating Centre for Humanitarian Assistance on disaster management), established in November 2011, is the primary regional coordinating agency responsible for operational coordination and facilitation among member states, UN entities, and international organizations. The ASEAN Emergency Response and Assessment Team (ERAT) acts as the official resource for rapid deployment under AADMER. AADMER member states include Brunei Darussalam, Cambodia, Indonesia, Lao PDR, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, and Viet Nam.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the core support mechanisms provided by the Caribbean Disaster Emergency Management Agency (CDEMA) via its Regional Response Mechanism (RRM)?
The CDEMA Regional Response Mechanism (RRM) provides five primary support mechanisms: 1) Rapid Needs Assessment Team (RNAT), which performs damage and impact assessments within 72 hours of deployment. 2) CARICOM Damage Assessment Coordination (CDAC), which increases national capacity for Damage Assessment and Needs Analysis (DANA) using methodologies tailored to the Caribbean. 3) CARICOM Operational Support Team (COST), which provides surge capacity for National Emergency Operations Centres (NEOC). 4) CARICOM Disaster Relief Unit (CDRU), a specialized unit of military, fire, and police assets from the 18 participating states tasked with relief supply management, emergency telecommunications, and repairing critical infrastructure. 5) Regional Search and Rescue Team (RSART), composed of six-person teams trained for urban light-level search operations to assist local authorities.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the operational purpose and scope of the ASEAN Emergency Response and Assessment Team (ERAT)?
ASEAN ERAT consists of trained, rapidly deployable experts tasked with assisting National Disaster Management Organizations (NDMOs) during the earliest phase of an emergency. Their specific operational responsibilities include: (a) conducting rapid assessments; (b) estimating the scale, severity, and impact of the disaster through damage assessment and needs analysis; (c) gathering and reporting on the immediate needs of the affected population; and (d) coordinating with the AHA Centre to facilitate the mobilization, deployment, and response of regional assets, capacities, and humanitarian goods. ERAT members are sourced from NDMOs, government ministries, the private sector, and civil society, totaling approximately 200 members as of 2017. They operate under guidelines modeled on UNDAC standards.

chunks.content:
P: What are the primary operational limitations of handheld satellite telephones during UNDAC missions?
When utilizing handheld satellite telephones during an UNDAC mission, operators must account for six critical operational limitations: 1) Indoor coverage is impossible without the use of an external antenna. 2) Coverage is not universal; not all satellite systems provide full global connectivity. 3) Network acquisition is not instantaneous and can require several minutes to establish a connection. 4) Data capabilities are extremely limited compared to terrestrial networks. 5) Large-scale emergencies often trigger bandwidth congestion, degrading service quality. 6) Operational costs are significant, generally costing at least US$1 per minute. Operators must also recognize that while the UN utilizes Iridium, Thuraya, and Inmarsat systems, there is no single standard model, requiring familiarity with the specific equipment provided in service packages.

queries.query: How should an UNDAC Information Management (IM) Cell handle the distribution of sensitive data versus data that benefits the whole humanitarian community?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should sensitive versus non-sensitive humanitarian data be shared according to best practices?
Data handling and sharing strategies are determined by the sensitivity of the information. Sensitive data must only be shared within the UNDAC Management System (UMS) and should utilize comprehensive naming conventions for all files. Conversely, information deemed of benefit to the broader humanitarian community and not classified as confidential should be disseminated through platforms such as HDX for data, or the Virtual OSOCC (VO) and HR.info for general information. If information is incomplete or potentially outdated, teams should not refrain from sharing it; instead, they should include a simple disclaimer within the dataset or report that explicitly mentions the known shortfalls. The UNDAC team is responsible for driving these practices and distributing standardized templates to partners for reporting response data, such as 3W (Who is doing What and Where) information, all of which are accessible via the UMS.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What information should the UNDAC team analyze and report to support humanitarian situational awareness?
The UNDAC team must ensure that data gathered through EMT coordination is utilized for broader situational analysis and priority setting. Key focus areas include: 1) The impact on the national health system. 2) The status of health facilities, specifically comparing the number of functioning facilities versus those that are damaged or non-functioning. 3) An inventory of all deployed national and international EMTs, including their specific numbers and types. 4) Any evidence of disease outbreaks. Furthermore, the UNDAC team is responsible for sharing logistical and operational arrangements, such as transport and refueling details, and ensuring regular information exchange between the EMTCC and the OSOCC A&A (Assessment and Analysis) Cell.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How can the UNDAC team support the EMTCC in information management?
The UNDAC team provides support to the EMTCC through several specific actions: 1) Leveraging expertise in tools like the Virtual On-Site Operations Coordination Centre (VOSOCC) to assist in interpreting and managing data, particularly within the 'relief teams' section. 2) Identifying specific team members or OSOCC Information Support Staff (OISS)—ideally those with previous EMTCC training—to function within the EMTCC information management unit. 3) Assisting in the development and distribution of a standard direct observation form, created by the OSOCC A&A Cell, and ensuring that findings regarding the status of health facilities are communicated back to the EMTCC on a regular basis to maintain situational awareness.

Information beneficial to the broader community should be disseminated through platforms like HDX for data, or the Virtual OSOCC (VO) and HR.info for general information. The UNDAC team drives these practices by distributing standardized templates for reporting response data.

queries.query: What are the specific requirements for requesting an Emergency Cash Grant, and how does this mechanism differ from the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF)?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the scope and operational criteria for the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF)?
The CERF is a specialized funding mechanism available exclusively to UN agencies to promote early action and reduce loss of life. To qualify for CERF funding, projects must strictly adhere to 'life-saving criteria.' This includes activities that directly remedy, mitigate, or avert physical harm and threats to a population, or support common humanitarian services, such as logistics, necessary to enable those life-saving operations. Requests are prepared by the RC/HC's office with support from OCHA (regional or country office) and submitted to the CERF Secretariat in New York. If no OCHA field presence exists, UNDAC teams are tasked with supporting the preparation of the rapid response submission under guidance from the regional office.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the criteria and procedural requirements for requesting an Emergency Cash Grant?
The Emergency Cash Grant is a small-scale financial tool used to kick-start humanitarian relief and coordination during the early stages of a disaster. Key parameters include: a maximum request limit of US$100,000 per grant; usage restricted to the procurement of relief supplies or the transport of such items; and a streamlined approval process. To secure these funds, the Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC) must submit a formal recommendation to OCHA Headquarters. Upon approval of this recommendation, funds are typically disbursed within a few days, allowing for immediate operational mobilization.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the operational procedure and purpose regarding the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF) for disaster response?
The CERF provides an immediate 'jump-start' of resources for critical life-saving activities while long-term funds are mobilized. It is not designed to cover all project requirements or replace an appeal; rather, it interacts with the appeal process to bridge the time lag between the issuance of an appeal and the receipt of donor commitments. Agency proposals for CERF must be endorsed by the RC/HC, as agencies cannot submit directly to the ERC. Proposals must be submitted as a package to the ERC and the CERF Secretariat at cerf@un.org, utilizing the official CERF budget template and a mandatory project allocation table. The RC/HC determines the allocation of funds to the most urgent, high-priority projects. Ideally, the appeal and CERF funding request should be prepared simultaneously, with CERF allocations clearly indicated in the appeal's summary financial tables.

queries.query: Describe the sequence of actions an UNDAC team must take if they encounter a severely dehydrated team member who has had diarrhea for two days in the field without professional medical help.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the correct sequence of operations when identifying and proposing a response activity during a humanitarian mission?
The A&A process explicitly warns that its primary purpose is not to identify an intervention directly, but rather to describe the most urgent problems and their underlying causes. Proposing specific response activities should only occur after the problems have been clearly identified through the analysis process. When suggesting a response, operators must take into account several critical factors: lessons learned from previous or current operations, existing national and international response capacities, recovery plans, supply pipelines, and current access constraints. This sequence ensures that recommendations are evidence-based and operationally viable rather than reactive. The focus remains on describing the situation so that strategic decision-makers can formulate appropriate interventions based on analyzed needs, rather than jumping prematurely to activity proposals without a comprehensive understanding of the humanitarian context and the operational constraints inherent in the disaster-affected environment.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What operational status must UNDAC teams maintain when deploying to an emergency site?
UNDAC teams deploying to an emergency must be fully operational and self-sufficient from the moment they land in the affected country. This is crucial because, immediately following a disaster, the team may encounter severely damaged infrastructure and communications, unsafe or destroyed buildings, and a serious disruption to daily life, facilities, and services.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What proactive measures should an UNDAC member take to enhance personal security and maintain 'street wise' situational awareness?
To maintain situational awareness and 'street wise' security, an operator must be constantly aware of their surroundings and react to potential threats before they escalate. It is critical to observe local behavior and routines; specifically, monitor the local population for changes in normal habits, as these shifts often serve as indicators of imminent instability, such as shelling or outbreaks of civil unrest. Furthermore, operators must strictly avoid predictable daily routines, as patterns allow potential aggressors to plan attacks more effectively. Always vary travel routes and timing when moving between fixed locations like living quarters and the base. When outside the base, you must utilize a 'buddy system' and never travel alone. Additionally, before entering the field, consult individuals who have recently traversed the same route to gather real-time security data, and consider documenting reported incidents on a map to maintain a clear picture of the threat environment.

queries.query: How does the operational structure of an OSOCC change when transitioning from Phase I (virtual activation) to Phase II (on-site arrival) during a large-scale emergency?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should the OSOCC structure be managed during a disaster?
The OSOCC structure must remain flexible to adapt to specific operational requirements. Depending on the event's magnitude, situational demands, and available resources, one person may manage multiple functions simultaneously, or larger functions may require a higher complement of personnel. In large-scale emergencies, a separate function may be established for Humanitarian Programme Cycle (HPC) support to manage humanitarian financing and cross-cutting issues such as gender, cash transfer programming (CTP), community engagement, and protection. An organizational chart displaying reporting lines must be developed, displayed within the OSOCC, and refreshed regularly to reflect the expansion or contraction of the OSOCC to meet evolving operational needs.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What does the acronym OSOCC stand for in the context of site management?
OSOCC stands for On-Site Operations Coordination Centre. It serves as the primary coordination hub for international search and rescue and humanitarian assistance teams deployed to a disaster site. The OSOCC acts as the interface between the international humanitarian community and local authorities, facilitating the sharing of information, the management of resources, and the synchronization of response activities to avoid duplication and ensure effective field operations during the initial phase of an emergency.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the primary operational function of an OSOCC in a disaster zone?
The OSOCC (On-Site Operations Coordination Centre) acts as a conduit for information exchange between the government of the affected country and international relief providers. It provides a platform for coordination among actors who do not typically work together. The facility facilitates on-site coordination and extends information exchange beyond the physical location of the OSOCC itself. To maximize effectiveness, it should be established immediately after a disaster requiring international assistance or when there is a significant shift in the emergency situation. This is particularly critical in sudden-onset disasters to ensure optimal rescue efforts and efficient resource management.

queries.query: What specific details must be included in the "Response strategy" and "Sector strategies" sections of a Flash Appeal document?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the essential elements required in the 'Sector Strategies' portion of a Flash Appeal?
In a Flash Appeal, sector-specific strategies must detail the following for every cluster: 1) Response objectives and identified priorities; 2) Target group considerations, including the total number of people intended for assistance; 3) Total consolidated financial requirements; and 4) A catalogue of response projects. This project catalogue should specify the individual organization and a designated contact person, a summary of the project’s specific objectives, activities, and priorities, as well as the financial requirements for each individual project.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the key elements included in a Flash Appeal document?
A Flash Appeal document consists of three primary components: 1) A Country Strategy, which provides an overview of the crisis, its scope and severity, and the initial government and bilateral response. 2) Strategic Objectives, which define the scope, timeframe, and target population groups based on needs analysis, government capacity, access constraints, and existing humanitarian efforts by other actors like the Red Cross or MSF. 3) A Response Strategy, which outlines anticipated risks, implementation considerations for overcoming obstacles, agreed priorities, and consolidated financial requirements. Additionally, the appeal includes Sector Strategies for each cluster, detailing response objectives, target figures, financial requirements, and, if possible, a catalogue of response projects by individual organizations.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Who is responsible for initiating a Flash Appeal and what is the process for its approval?
The Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC) initiates the Flash Appeal. This action must be taken in consultation with the Humanitarian Country Team (HCT) and is subject to the agreement of the Emergency Relief Coordinator (ERC). While government permission is not required to issue the appeal, consultation with national authorities is considered essential. Engagement with national authorities usually occurs through cluster or sector discussions with the relevant line ministries.

queries.query: If an international military force is deployed to assist in disaster relief, what five specific steps must the UN-CMCoord assessment take to determine the appropriate liaison strategy?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the five essential steps for conducting a UN-CMCoord assessment?
UN-CMCoord assessment defines the operational environment and should ideally commence at the pre-deployment phase using global contacts and secondary data analysis if an OCHA country office presence is unavailable. The assessment process is structured around five core pillars that must be analyzed to determine the most effective liaison strategy: 1) The Operating environment; 2) Key actors, including an evaluation of their specific missions and mandates; 3) Relationships, including current approaches and local perceptions; 4) Existing coordination mechanisms already in play; and 5) The availability of specific military assets that can be utilized in support of humanitarian action. Each of these five steps is considered equally vital to the integrity of the civil-military coordination strategy.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the five essential steps for assessing the operating environment during the UN-CMCoord assessment process?
The assessment process for humanitarian civil-military coordination requires evaluating five specific areas to determine the appropriate liaison strategy. First, assess the operating environment as a whole. Second, identify all relevant actors, which includes documenting their specific missions and mandates. Third, analyze the relationships, approaches, and perceptions held by these actors. Fourth, identify and map existing coordination mechanisms. Fifth, inventory all available military assets that could potentially support humanitarian action. Each of these steps is considered equally important for establishing an effective civil-military coordination foundation. These assessments help in compiling an inventory of key and supporting actors, existing structures, and potential modes of interface between international humanitarian actors and both domestic and international military entities.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What protocols must be followed to ensure the proper deployment and utilization of Foreign Military Assets (FMA) in a disaster zone?
The use of FMA must be based on clearly identified humanitarian gaps and appropriate categories of assistance tasks. Prior to deployment, governments should consult the Emergency Relief Coordinator (ERC) to ensure the mission is properly formulated and that the military force has the appropriate capacity to deploy. Once in-country, FMA must seek guidance from the Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC) and OCHA. Furthermore, FMA must maintain designated, trained liaison officers who are responsible for interacting directly with UN-CMCoord Officers on the ground. These protocols help mitigate risks to the perceived neutrality, impartiality, and operational independence of the humanitarian effort, ensuring that military assets are used optimally in support of established humanitarian priorities.
