queries.query: What is the exact maximum weight capacity (in kilograms) for the standard UNDAC issued BGAN terminal carrying case?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the standard conversion formulas for calculating weight and liquid measurements in the field?
For weight, the conversions are: 1 gram equals 0.035 ounces; 1 ounce equals 28.3 grams; 1 kilogram equals 2.2 pounds; 1 pound equals 454 grams; 1 ton equals 2,200 pounds; 1 pound equals 0.454 kilograms. Regarding ton-to-ton (US) conversions, 1 ton equals 0.984 tons (US), and 1 ton (US) equals 1.02 tons. For liquid volume, the conversion factors are: 1 milliliter equals 0.035 fluid ounces; 1 fluid ounce equals 28.4 milliliters; 1 liter equals 1.76 pints; 1 pint equals 0.568 liters; 1 liter equals 0.22 UK gallons; 1 UK gallon equals 4.55 liters. For cross-referencing US and UK liquid units, 1 US gallon equals 0.833 UK gallons, and 1 UK gallon equals 1.2 US gallons.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the primary requirement for establishing a stable connection with a BGAN satellite terminal in the field?
In the initial phase of an emergency where local telecommunications infrastructure is compromised or low-capacity, the Broadband Global Area Network (BGAN) system serves as the primary solution for data and telephone access. To ensure a stable and efficient connection, the most critical operational requirement is the precise physical orientation of the terminal's antenna. The antenna must be pointed directly towards the target satellite. Operators must understand that even though various models of BGAN terminals exist, they all function via similar principles. Ensuring the antenna maintains a clear line of sight to the satellite is essential for maintaining the link necessary to access email and critical online tools required for team operations.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What operational constraints and bandwidth management protocols should be observed when using BGAN terminals?
BGAN systems have a limited bandwidth of up to 0.5 Mbps, which is shared among all connected users and further shared via 'spot-beams' among users in the same geographical area. Given high operational costs—reaching up to US$6 per megabyte of data and US$16 per minute for streaming—strict bandwidth management is required. Operators must disable automatic computer updates and avoid non-essential activities such as watching video, downloading large email attachments, using social media feeds, or using Skype unless strictly necessary. These measures are critical, particularly in large-scale emergencies where limited satellite capacity is shared across multiple actors. When BGAN capacity or cost is prohibitive, personnel should evaluate existing local infrastructure or, for expanding missions, request a VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal) through partners like the IHP or TSF, noting that VSAT requires specially trained personnel for installation.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the mandatory health screening components for pre-deployment of UNDAC members?
To ensure UNDAC members remain in the best health to face emergency response challenges, they must undergo regular health screenings. These must include, at a minimum: a general medical examination which incorporates both blood and urine tests; a chest X-ray; an electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG); a comprehensive dental examination; an eye examination; and gender-specific screenings such as a breast examination and Pap test where applicable. Additionally, personnel must verify that all vaccinations are up-to-date according to the standards outlined in Section C.2.1 of the manual. These screenings are critical for the early detection and effective management of medical conditions before deployment.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What items must every UNDAC member carry, and what are the guidelines for preparing the medical kit?
Every UNDAC member is mandated to carry a personal medical kit to manage minor health illnesses or injuries. This kit must be prepared in advance, requiring periodic checking of all included medicine expiry dates. The contents must be clearly marked, specifying the name of the medication and proper instructions for its usage. For optimal organization, a sturdy, waterproof container with segregated compartments is recommended. Suggested contents must cover minor health and injury treatment.

queries.query: If a Heavy USAR team is deploying to a Level 5 radiation zone, what specific protective suits are required by the INSARAG guidelines?

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the specific scope of structural search and rescue capabilities for an INSARAG Light USAR team?
A Light USAR team is equipped and trained to conduct both technical and/or canine search and rescue operations specifically within collapsed structures composed of wood, masonry, and light reinforced concrete. Regarding physical limitations and structural materials, the team is capable of handling the following: timber up to 200 mm, reinforcing bars (rebar), non-structural mesh reinforcing, and manual rigging/lifting up to 1 Tonne using levers. Unlike Medium or Heavy teams, Light teams are not required to manage concrete columns or beams. While they must maintain all core USAR components, their primary operational capability is focused on ASR3 (Assessment, Search, and Rescue) tasks at their allocated worksite, operating under the established INSARAG classification framework.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the operational requirements and staffing standards for an INSARAG Light USAR team?
A Light USAR team must be supported by their National Policy Focal Point as a deployable resource with appropriate funding. Teams can be sourced from existing national capacities or NGOs. Staffing typically consists of 17 to 20 personnel, with the flexibility to combine positions for efficiency. Critically, the team must provide all five USAR components: Management, Search, Rescue, Logistics, and Medical. They must be self-sufficient on a single worksite for 12-hour work shifts followed by 12-hour rest cycles over a 5-day duration. The team must have independent transport capabilities for international deployment and provide one person to support the Reception/Departure Centre (RDC) or the USAR Coordination Cell (UCC) for the mission's duration. They are specifically authorized to conduct ASR3 level operations and must integrate into standard INSARAG reporting mechanisms.

chunks.title: INSARAG20Guidelines20Vol20II2C20Man20A.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the performance criteria and required equipment for a First Responder in the INSARAG framework?
A First Responder must be trained in structural systems (wood, light metal, masonry, adobe, or bamboo), hazardous materials response, medical first response, Incident Command Systems (ICS), basic USAR, and INSARAG guidelines. Performance criteria include the ability to establish or assume command within an ICS, recognize risks in light-frame structures, and maintain international safety standards. Personnel must perform rescues involving light rubble removal, load-lifting via levers and cribbing, and basic emergency medical care (stabilization, immobilization, and extrication). They must also apply the INSARAG Marking System, conduct basic searches, and manage hazmat incidents. Required equipment includes basic cutting and breaking tools, ropes of various thicknesses with accessories, assorted levers, and cribbing supplies. Communication equipment must be sufficient for the team size and search tasks. Furthermore, the cache must include basic life-support equipment, personal protective equipment (PPE), signaling devices such as whistles or horns, marking supplies, and ABC fire extinguishers.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How does OCHA deploy UNDAC regional teams, and when is a global deployment necessary?
The UNDAC team structure is categorized into several regional teams: Africa, Middle East and Europe; The Americas (including the Caribbean); and Asia and the Pacific. For emergencies that are primarily of a national or regional concern, OCHA generally utilizes the regional UNDAC team members corresponding to the affected region. This strategic deployment ensures that OCHA can mobilize emergency managers who are highly familiar with the local culture, language, and specific context. Conversely, for major emergencies that demand large or multiple deployments, OCHA has the capacity to draw upon UNDAC members from all regions worldwide to compose the required teams.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How does OCHA Geneva contribute to the reporting process at the end of an UNDAC mission?
In addition to the primary mission report generated by the team, OCHA Geneva prepares a separate, short end-of-mission report. This document focuses on capturing key highlights from the deployment. The primary intent of this report is to facilitate communication with the specific government or organization that sponsored the UNDAC team member. Furthermore, this document is officially distributed during the annual UNDAC Advisory Board meeting to ensure transparency and institutional oversight of mission outcomes and operational performance.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the specific requirements for photos provided by the UNDAC team for OCHA use?
The UNDAC communications focal point must ensure that all photos provided are of high quality and document the disaster context, including the extent and severity of the crisis, damage, affected people, UNDAC in action, and wider response activities. Each photograph must be accompanied by a comprehensive caption. This caption is required to include specific details regarding where the photo was taken, when it was taken, who is depicted in the image, and the name of the photographer. Once these photos are submitted to the mission focal point in Geneva, OCHA assumes the responsibility of determining how and when these assets will be used. Capturing visuals during the initial days and weeks of a disaster is prioritized, as these images are critical for OCHA's advocacy and resource mobilization efforts.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What services are provided by coordination platforms in complex settings like peacekeeping, peace enforcement, or combat environments?
In high-stakes environments such as peacekeeping or combat, civil-military constituents utilize structures like UN-CMCoord Cells, Working Groups, Advisory Groups, or Forums. These provide key services including: sharing information for common situational awareness regarding humanitarian activities, safety, security, access, logistics, and communications; establishing humanitarian notification systems for deconfliction; and the coherent use of military assets (including those from UN missions) to support humanitarian action. These platforms also manage humanitarian sensitization, such as training events, workshops, and briefings. They contribute to critical protection-of-civilians efforts and maintain rigorous documentation and reporting of all civil-military interactions. In all such circumstances, close collaboration with the Logistics Cluster is mandatory to coordinate the use of military logistics assets.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the criteria and procedural requirements for requesting an Emergency Cash Grant?
The Emergency Cash Grant is a small-scale financial tool used to kick-start humanitarian relief and coordination during the early stages of a disaster. Key parameters include: a maximum request limit of US$100,000 per grant; usage restricted to the procurement of relief supplies or the transport of such items; and a streamlined approval process. To secure these funds, the Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC) must submit a formal recommendation to OCHA Headquarters. Upon approval of this recommendation, funds are typically disbursed within a few days, allowing for immediate operational mobilization.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the priority measures for protecting property rights and land assets of vulnerable groups following a disaster?
Protecting assets for vulnerable groups—including older persons, those with disabilities, child-headed households, and vulnerable women—requires a multi-faceted approach. Agencies should utilize asset registrars and active monitoring to maintain oversight. Ownership must be secured by registering inherited land or property specifically in the owner’s name, rather than the name of a guardian or deceased relative, and by utilizing joint registration for matrimonial property. To combat harmful practices such as widow chasing or ritual cleansing that deny property rights, legal aid and information programs must be established. Technical efforts should include securing land records through digitization or freeze-dry techniques. Furthermore, authorities should advocate for flexibility in evidence requirements, such as allowing community-based identification verification or accepting customary marriage status to prevent the exclusion of women from restitution programs. Finally, efforts must include renters, squatters, and the landless in reconstruction and housing schemes to ensure equitable support.

The OSOCC (On-Site Operations Coordination Centre) acts as a conduit for information exchange between the government of the affected country and international relief providers. chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the primary role of the OSOCC in relation to the Local Emergency Management Authority (LEMA)?
The OSOCC operates in support of the affected government to coordinate international response organizations. Within the host country, the LEMA holds the responsibility for the overall command, coordination, and management of the response operation. Consequently, the OSOCC is required to maintain a strong, continuous connection with the LEMA throughout the duration of the mission. The OSOCC also provides support and collaborates with cluster coordinators and responding teams, which can be achieved through integration into the OSOCC structure—such as physically locating them within the facility—or through the establishment of formal or informal liaison channels.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the scope of the Management Function within an OSOCC?
The Management Function is responsible for coordinating the efforts of all other OSOCC functions. Its primary tasks include establishing routines for internal information flow, setting up formal liaison with national authorities and response organizations, and ensuring the safety and security of international responders. The function is led by an OSOCC Manager, who is responsible for providing leadership, conducting internal briefings, and managing task allocation among personnel. The Manager ensures that the OSOCC meets objectives and fulfills the Terms of Reference (ToR) established by the affected country's government, the UNDAC Team Leader, and the Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator (RC/HC). Specific responsibilities for cells under this function are detailed in position checklists available on the VOSOCC website.

queries.query: What are the required dimensions (length and width) for the designated helicopter landing pad inside an OSOCC base camp?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the operational difference between 'hovering in ground effect' (HIGE) and 'hovering out of ground effect' (HOGE) regarding helicopter payload planning?
The payload capacity of a helicopter varies significantly based on the flight environment. 'Hovering in ground effect' (HIGE) payloads should be used when takeoff and landing areas are relatively flat and the cargo is non-jettisonable. In contrast, 'hovering out of ground effect' (HOGE) payloads must be utilized for sling load missions—where cargo is suspended from a belly hook via nets or lines—and for operations in adverse terrain, such as landing on top of steep ridges or adjacent to cliffs, or when dealing with adverse weather conditions. Operators must note that these figures are strictly guidelines, as actual payload capacity for both modes will fluctuate based on critical variables including current elevation, ambient air temperature, the amount of fuel currently on board, and other environmental factors.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: Which aircraft from the provided list can transport the highest quantity of 224 x 318 cm pallets, and what is its runway requirement?
The Boeing 747-100F and 747-200F are the most capable aircraft listed for palletized cargo, both accommodating 37 pallets measuring 224 x 318 cm. The B747-100F requires a desired runway length of 9,000 ft, while the B747-200F requires a longer runway of 10,700 ft. Other high-capacity options include the DC-10-10F and DC-10-30F, which accommodate 23 pallets and require 8,000 ft of runway. When selecting these aircraft, ensure the airfield infrastructure matches or exceeds these runway length requirements to ensure safe landing and takeoff operations under emergency load conditions.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the primary considerations for designing an OSOCC that includes staff accommodation?
If an OSOCC incorporates staff accommodation, the specific tented areas must be physically secluded from the rest of the facility to ensure they are not accessible to the public. In large-scale emergencies, planners should proactively prepare for significant growth, as OCHA surge capacity and other international organizations will require additional office space and service areas shortly after deployment. It is tactically superior to plan for larger spatial requirements than initially anticipated to avoid operational bottlenecks caused by limited availability. When designing these environments, operators should utilize the base camp site selection checklists, as the logistical and environmental considerations—such as layout, security, and infrastructure requirements—are largely interchangeable between OSOCC tented configurations and dedicated base camps.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How can an UNDAC team conduct mapping if no dedicated GIS team or specialized equipment is available?
If a dedicated mapping or GIS team is unavailable, teams can utilize basic, accessible tools such as Google Earth or PowerPoint. Even simple, manual methods such as creating hand-drawn overlays on photocopied road maps are considered adequate for communicating essential aspects of the operational picture. Additionally, teams should attempt to request that a GIS unit provide pre-prepared PowerPoint map sets; these can then be edited by non-GIS experts to generate situation maps for reports. Offline capabilities are supported by caching or saving map data from Google Maps/Earth in advance, or by cutting and pasting map areas of interest from OpenStreetMap (OSM) into software like PowerPoint for use in environments without internet access.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should sensitive versus non-sensitive humanitarian data be shared according to best practices?
Data handling and sharing strategies are determined by the sensitivity of the information. Sensitive data must only be shared within the UNDAC Management System (UMS) and should utilize comprehensive naming conventions for all files. Conversely, information deemed of benefit to the broader humanitarian community and not classified as confidential should be disseminated through platforms such as HDX for data, or the Virtual OSOCC (VO) and HR.info for general information. If information is incomplete or potentially outdated, teams should not refrain from sharing it; instead, they should include a simple disclaimer within the dataset or report that explicitly mentions the known shortfalls. The UNDAC team is responsible for driving these practices and distributing standardized templates to partners for reporting response data, such as 3W (Who is doing What and Where) information, all of which are accessible via the UMS.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the purpose of a 3W product and how can it be created?
The 3W product (Who is doing What and Where) is designed to document the operational presence of humanitarian partners broken down by sector and geographic location during an emergency. The flexibility of the 3W allows it to be produced in various formats depending on the UNDAC team's current technical capacity and resources. At a basic level, it can be created as a simple table within an Excel spreadsheet. If resources and data permit, the team can produce more sophisticated 3W products, such as maps that visually plot the specific presence and activities of operational partners in the field.

EMTs must adhere to specific core standards regarding information management: they must register with the relevant national authority or lead international agency upon arrival. They must report their specific type, capacity, and services offered based on the international classification system. During the response phase, EMTs must report at regular intervals, and they are required to provide a final report prior to departure. These reports should utilize national formats if available; otherwise, they must use the agreed-upon international reporting format. chunks.title: INSARAG-Guidelines-V2-Manual-B-Operations.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What information must be provided to the OSOCC/UC upon the demobilization of a USAR team?
Upon demobilization, a USAR team must complete and submit a formal Demobilization Form to the On-Site Operations Coordination Centre (OSOCC) or the UCC. This document serves as the official record of the team's departure. The required data points include the team's identity (Team ID and Team Name), specific departure logistics (date, time, and the physical point of departure), and comprehensive transport and flight information. Additionally, the team must report their operational footprint by providing the total weight (in tons) and volume (in cubic meters) of their equipment. Personnel and logistical tracking are also mandatory, requiring the total count of team members and the number of search dogs deployed. The form also captures specific administrative needs to facilitate the transition, including requests for ground transportation, assistance with loading or unloading equipment, and any requirements for accommodation at the point of departure. The document must be validated with the name, title/position of the official completing the form, and the date of submission.

Furthermore, the UNDAC team should encourage the MoH to assign a dedicated liaison person to the RDC. Ideally, this liaison should either manage the RDC for EMTs directly or at least provide the necessary coordination bridge to ensure that arriving teams are properly recorded, briefed, and directed to the EMTCC for final registration and authorization to operate.

queries.query: According to the guidelines, what specific psychological test must be administered during the "Exploration phase" of a defusing session?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the core goals and procedural guidelines for conducting a 'defusing' session?
Defusing is a peer-led intervention for staff members exposed to a traumatic event who remain in control of their emotions. It should not be forced upon anyone, especially within the first few hours post-incident. The primary goals are to provide information about the incident, allow for the sharing of perceptions and stress reactions, reinforce mutual support to dispel feelings of uniqueness, and identify which colleagues require professional follow-up services. The session should be facilitated as a free-flowing conversation rather than a rigid interrogation. It is critical to distinguish 'defusing' from 'psychological debriefing,' as the former is designed for non-mental health professionals to help normalize stress responses, whereas debriefing is a specific clinical process conducted by mental health professionals.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the three main phases of a 'defusing' session after a traumatic event?
Defusing consists of three interconnected phases designed as a free-flowing conversation: 1) Introduction phase (5-10 minutes): Team members are introduced, the intervention process is explained, and session expectations are established. 2) Exploration phase (10-30 minutes): This is the core time for discussing the traumatic experience. Participants disclose facts, share cognitive and emotional reactions, and identify specific symptoms experienced since the event. 3) Information phase (5-15 minutes): This phase focuses on educating participants about traumatic stress. It is crucial to note that these steps do not need to follow a rigid sequence; the flow should remain natural and prioritize the evolving needs of the staff members involved.

queries.query: Which specific model of Thuraya satellite phone is issued as standard equipment in the IHP service package?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the primary operational limitations of handheld satellite telephones during UNDAC missions?
When utilizing handheld satellite telephones during an UNDAC mission, operators must account for six critical operational limitations: 1) Indoor coverage is impossible without the use of an external antenna. 2) Coverage is not universal; not all satellite systems provide full global connectivity. 3) Network acquisition is not instantaneous and can require several minutes to establish a connection. 4) Data capabilities are extremely limited compared to terrestrial networks. 5) Large-scale emergencies often trigger bandwidth congestion, degrading service quality. 6) Operational costs are significant, generally costing at least US$1 per minute. Operators must also recognize that while the UN utilizes Iridium, Thuraya, and Inmarsat systems, there is no single standard model, requiring familiarity with the specific equipment provided in service packages.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the common methods for delivering Cash Transfer Programmes (CTP) in the field?
There are six primary methods for CTP delivery: 1) Direct cash payment: Hard currency is distributed directly to beneficiaries by the implementing organization or partners. 2) Delivery through agents: Cash is routed through local financial service providers, such as money transfer agents, post offices, traders, banks, or microfinance institutions. 3) Pre-paid cards: ATM, debit, or prepaid cards, which require internet or phone connectivity and are issued by bank or non-bank providers. 4) Physical vouchers: Non-electronic tokens or paper vouchers. 5) Mobile phone transfers: Money or vouchers sent via mobile network operators or banks. 6) Smart cards (chipped): Cards designed for cash grants and store purchases that do not require an active internet or phone line connection during use.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What protocol should be followed regarding mobile phones and data consumption during field missions?
Operators should be aware that mobile phone compatibility varies by region due to local GSM standard configurations, operator locking, or geographic restrictions. To avoid excessive roaming costs, particularly for mobile data, it is recommended to utilize local SIM cards provided by mission support staff, which also facilitates better communication with local partners. When using smartphones on local 3/4G networks or OSOCC WLANs, users must disable automatic application updates and push notifications to minimize bandwidth load. If a phone is brought from home, confirm it is not locked to a specific carrier and be prepared to rely on local equipment if the home device fails to function within the specific regional infrastructure.

queries.query: What is the exact mathematical formula used by the Logistics Cluster to forecast commercial supply chain demand in a disaster?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the primary characteristics that distinguish humanitarian supply chains from commercial supply chains during a disaster?
Humanitarian supply chains differ significantly from commercial systems due to their inherent unpredictability. Unlike commercial chains that utilize mathematical models and forecasting, humanitarian logistics operate with unknown timings, commodities, and geography. They are established on very short lead times under high-stakes conditions where the primary objectives include saving lives, managing media scrutiny, and maintaining donor accountability. Furthermore, they are frequently constrained by a critical lack of initial resources required for successful implementation in the early stages of a disaster response.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the core functional elements of a disaster logistics system?
A functional disaster logistics system must provide for the delivery of appropriate supplies in good condition to the required location and time. It necessitates a system for prioritizing relief inputs and managing bulk commodities, which includes storing, staging, and moving goods. Furthermore, the system must facilitate the movement of people and the coordination and prioritization of limited, shared transport assets, which are often improvised at the local level. Logistics planning must also account for limited, rapid deliveries from outside the area and the potential involvement of military forces, especially during civil conflicts.

P: What is the scope and purpose of the FRANZ Agreement in Pacific disaster response?
The France, Australia, and New Zealand (FRANZ) Agreement, signed in 1992, serves as a regional disaster response mechanism for Pacific Island countries. It commits these three nations to coordinate, share information, and ensure the optimal use of assets when responding to natural disasters. The agreement includes the provision of resources, comprising civil, commercial, and military personnel. The three partner countries meet annually to review and discuss their disaster response mechanisms and to conduct coordination practice drills. Militaries under this framework play a critical role in providing unique assets and specialist expertise, specifically for conducting aerial and sea surveillance and assisting in the transportation and distribution of essential relief items when national capacities are overwhelmed.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: How should UNDAC members define the scope of their logistics responsibilities when deployed to a disaster zone?
UNDAC members are typically deployed early and must be prepared to either initiate logistics arrangements or advise on the planning and implementation of basic logistics programmes. Their responsibilities generally fall into three operational levels: 1) Internal logistics support for the UNDAC team itself (e.g., transport, office set-up, and field preparation handled via the OSOCC Support Function). 2) Logistics support for emergency response teams coordinated by the OSOCC, such as USAR or EMT, which involves orienting teams on local accommodation and provider availability. 3) General logistics support for the wider humanitarian community, serving as part of UNDAC’s role in situational awareness and information management. In rare instances, operational activities may be requested by the RC/HC, such as using an OCHA Emergency Cash Grant to secure essential contracts.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the specific duties of an UNDAC Team Leader serving as an OCHA Security Focal Point in countries without an OCHA office?
When no OCHA office exists in-country, the UNDAC Team Leader acts as the OCHA Security Focal Point to the Security Management Team (SMT). Their duties include serving as a temporary SMT member, attending all SMT meetings and training sessions, and keeping all UNDAC staff fully informed of security-related information and measures. They must ensure a fully functional, operational communications system for security management within the UNDAC team that is integrated into the UN Country Emergency Communications System. If UNDAC staff are assigned as Wardens or Area Security Coordinators, the Team Leader must ensure they receive adequate support and time for required training. Finally, they are responsible for reporting all instances of security policy non-compliance by UNDAC staff members to the Designated Official (DO) and UNDSS, while taking appropriate follow-up actions as necessary.

queries.query: According to the manual, what is the exact dosage of antibiotics recommended for treating Schistosomiasis (snail fever) in the field?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What primary health and hygiene protocols should UNDAC members follow to maintain operational readiness while on mission?
To maintain operational readiness, members must prioritize rest, diet, and hygiene. Even with demanding schedules, team members should secure regular sleep, as a sick member becomes a liability rather than an asset. Diets should be well-balanced; avoid heavy meals and strictly moderate alcohol consumption to evenings only. Increase liquid intake to compensate for perspiration, and increase salt intake if sweating is profuse. Practice strict hygiene, especially frequent hand-washing before meals. All water intended for oral or dental hygiene must be boiled or purified. To prevent parasitic infections like Schistosomiasis (snail fever), avoid swimming or bathing in lakes, rivers, or standing water unless verified safe. Because newcomers are particularly vulnerable to local diseases due to lower resistance, these environmental monitoring and health measures are critical to preventing acute or chronic digestive illnesses and other infections.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the post-mission medical requirements for UNDAC team members?
Following a mission, personnel must monitor their health for signs of injury or illness and seek prompt medical consultation if symptoms appear. Specific concern is warranted for persistent fever, cough, or abdominal distress coupled with diarrhea, as these may indicate diseases contracted during deployment. Any medication regimens initiated during the mission, particularly anti-malarial drugs, must be completed as prescribed or according to manufacturer instructions found in the packaging. If an illness develops post-deployment, personnel are required to update their individual health records and notify OCHA. This notification is vital as it allows OCHA to alert other team members to potential health threats in the deployment area or inform local health authorities at the site.

chunks.title: UC_Handbook_2022-2.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the mandatory equipment list for an RDC / UCC team according to Annex E?
The RDC/UCC team equipment is categorized into several functional kits. Core site infrastructure includes INSARAG signs (UN flags/arrows), a tent, a generator with fuel, and lighting sets. Furnishings consist of sleeping bags (fold-up beds recommended), fold-up tables, and chairs. Administrative and communication supplies include a roll of twine, printed INSARAG forms, A3 information displays, whiteboards, flipcharts, and a comprehensive stationery kit (pens, pencils, highlighters, markers, 3 reams of A4 paper, clipboards, scissors, hooks, tape, and rulers). The communication kit is extensive, featuring laptops, a mobile printer with cartridges/cables, a scanner, mice, auxiliary batteries, a camera, USB sticks, mobile phones, GPS with 2GB micro-SD, a BGAN unit, satellite phones, and UHF portable radios with dual charging docks. Power management requires 4-outlet power boards, international adaptors, 20m leads, and safety switch boards. Finally, the team must be self-sufficient with a sanitary kit (toilet paper, field toilet liquid/seats, water purifiers, camp shower) and ration packs complete with heaters.

queries.query: Which private sector telecommunications company holds the exclusive global contract to provide WiFi routers for OSOCC tents?

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the due diligence and risk management requirements when engaging the private sector?
Engagement must be guided by UN guidelines on principle-based cooperation, which prioritize safeguarding the UN Charter. Personnel must conduct due diligence to avoid reputational risk, which precludes engaging with companies on UN sanctions lists, those involved in forced or child labor, or the weapons trade, and restricts engagement with sectors like gambling or tobacco. UNDAC members should distinguish between 'engagement' (information exchange/coordination) and 'partnership' (operational agreements managed by specific agencies). If a company offers direct support to UNDAC, personnel must contact OCHA Geneva for vetting. Generally, interacting with industry networks rather than individual local entities serves as a primary risk-mitigation strategy. If companies seek procurement opportunities, they must be directed exclusively to the UN Global Marketplace at https://www.ungm.org.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What are the recommended categories of private sector actors for an UNDAC team to target for collaboration?
To target private sector actors effectively, UNDAC should reach out to two main categories: Business Networks and individual Companies. Business Networks are prioritized as they allow for reaching multiple entities via one focal point. Examples include local or international Chambers of Commerce (which often include multinational members and can serve as conveners), Connecting Business initiative (CBi) local networks, UN Global Compact local networks, and other industry-specific networks. Individual companies of interest include global corporations with a local presence; those in sectors relevant to the local context such as ICT, telecommunications, financial services, and consumer industries; and companies based in neighboring countries with strong economies. Engaging with networks is generally preferred to individual companies to minimize potential due diligence and reputational risk issues.

chunks.title: 1823826E_web_pages.pdf
chunks.content:
P: What is the strategic value of engaging the private sector during humanitarian emergencies, and how should this be initiated?
The private sector often holds superior situational awareness, established supply chains, and technical resources compared to traditional humanitarian actors. Because private sector interest is typically highest during the first week of a crisis, UNDAC should engage them immediately to link their activities to humanitarian coordination structures. Engagement should focus on 'quick wins,' such as utilizing local resources or existing global agreements (e.g., Connecting Business initiative) to solve concrete operational issues like transport or infrastructure repair. To maximize reach, UNDAC should prioritize engaging business networks—such as Chambers of Commerce, CBi local networks, or UN Global Compact local networks—rather than individual companies, as this minimizes due diligence complexities. Always remind private sector partners of humanitarian principles and 'do no harm' protocols.
